narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, you've put significant effort into your application, but I understand how disappointing it must be to receive this rejection. The admissions model we use predicts your likelihood of failing the bar exam on your first try, and it estimated a 63.9% probability of failure for you—high enough that it tipped the decision against admitting you right now. This prediction stems from a careful look at key parts of your profile, like your undergraduate GPA of 2.40, your LSAT score of 27, and especially your school decile rankings from Year 1 (2nd decile) and Year 3 (1st decile), compared to typical students who average much higher in these areas—around 5th or 6th deciles and GPAs near 3.21 with LSATs around 37. The two biggest factors pushing the model toward predicting bar failure were your lower Year 3 and Year 1 decile rankings, as these stood out sharply below the strong academic performance seen in students who typically pass the bar. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA and LSAT score helped pull the prediction somewhat toward success, showing potential that countered the trend a bit, while your upper-middle income and full-time status provided smaller positive nudges, aligning with the majority of successful applicants. Overall, your profile compares to typical admitted students by lagging most in those early academic deciles, which the model weighed heavily as red flags for bar success. This isn't a judgment on your character or potential—many students overcome similar hurdles. To move forward, I recommend retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the average, strengthening your recent grades to improve your decile rankings, and perhaps gaining practical experience like internships to build a more competitive profile. Reapplying after addressing these areas could shift the model's view in your favor. You're capable of turning this around—keep pushing, and reach out to admissions advisors for personalized guidance on next steps. We're rooting for your success.
